title: Fore-edge painting, painting the fanned edge
subtitle: A painting hidden on the foredge, visible only when the book is fanned open
excerpt: Fore-edge painting hides a watercolour scene on the fanned foredge of a bound text block. When the book is closed, the painting is invisible. Fan the pages at a slight angle, hold them there, and the image appears. The technique dates from the English decorative bookbinding tradition of the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.
sourceNotes: Cyril Davenport, The Book, Its History and Development (1907), chapter on decorative bindings including gilt and painted foredges. Internet Archive. Samuel Mearne's bindings and the English cottage-style fore-edge paintings are discussed in general bibliophilic literature of the period.

glossaryTerms.definition: A watercolour or gouache painting applied to the fanned pages of a book's foredge, invisible when the book is closed.
The sewn and trimmed body of pages inside a binding, before the case or covers are attached.
Displacing each page of a text block a small distance from the one below it, so the foredge presents a stepped surface that can be painted on.

content.text: exploits one property of a bound book: when you fan the pages, the

reveals a stepped surface along the foredge. Paint on that surface and it vanishes when the pages fall back together. The technique flourished in England from the 1650s through the early nineteenth century, particularly on fine calf-bound octavo volumes.

The book should already be bound and cased. Choose one with a smooth trimmed foredge (not deckled) and at least 150 pages so the fanning reveals a wide enough surface to paint on. You will also need fine watercolour brushes (a size 2 and a size 0), artist watercolour, and a low-tack masking tape to protect the boards.

Open the book and lay it flat on a clean board. Fan the text block along the foredge by pressing on the board and allowing all pages to slide in the same direction. Aim for a fan angle of about 3-5 degrees: enough to reveal 5-8 mm of each leaf.
Once fanned, place the book spine-down in the nipping press with the fanned foredge uppermost and accessible. Tighten the press enough to hold the fan without crushing the pages. The
must stay constant throughout the painting session: any movement shifts the image.
Apply low-tack masking tape along the boards to protect them from watercolour splatter.

Sketch a very light pencil outline on the fanned surface. The image works best when the subject has both sky (or light background) at the top and a clear foreground at the bottom, a landscape, a harbour scene, a floral spray. Detailed architectural subjects are achievable but time-consuming.

Paint in broad washes first: sky, water, distant hills. Allow each wash to dry before working closer detail. The surface absorbs paint slightly differently depending on paper stock, so test a small area at the top or bottom margin before committing the full image.
Work foreground detail last with the finest brush. Keep the paint dry-ish: wet paint that seeps between pages will block them from closing cleanly.

Once the painting is completely dry, release the press slowly. The image will vanish as the pages fall together. Fan the book at the same angle to check register: the image should appear complete and in sharp focus.

items.symptom: The image is blurred or misregistered when the pages are fanned
items.cause: The fan angle shifted during painting.
items.fix: Mark the fan angle on the press with a pencil mark before releasing. At the next session, re-fan to exactly the same angle.

items.symptom: Pages stick together at the foredge
items.cause: Watercolour was applied too wet and wicked between pages.
items.fix: Allow each layer to dry thoroughly before adding the next. Fan the pages very gently once dry to break any incipient sticking.

items.cause: The paper is absorbent and the paint was laid unevenly.
items.fix: Lay in a clean water wash first and let it dry; this pre-dampens the surface evenly and reduces blotchiness. Some unevenness is a natural characteristic of the technique.
